DDale Anfer
Findings Notebook Next steps ← 返回主页

Technical article / HR analytics EDA

HR Analytics EDA: Employee Attrition and Workforce Insights

This project explores employee attrition patterns using a public HR analytics dataset. The goal is to understand which employee characteristics are associated with higher attrition risk and translate the findings into interpretable HR insights.

The project is for analytical and educational purposes only. It is not intended for automated hiring, firing, or employee evaluation decisions.

Dataset Kaggle HR Analytics Dataset Task Attrition EDA Methods Audit · Wilson CI · Cramér’s V · diagnostics Evidence Tables · figures · code review Boundary Descriptive, not causal

Reader map

Key findings before the notebook details

01Attrition is imbalanced

Only about 16% of employees left, so accuracy alone is not a useful modeling target.

02Overtime is the clearest categorical signal

Overtime employees show much higher observed attrition than non-overtime employees.

03Role, age, salary, travel, and marital status matter descriptively

These variables show meaningful marginal associations, but group size and confidence intervals matter.

04Numerical diagnostics support the same pattern

Leavers tend to be younger, lower-income, farther from work, and shorter-tenured.

05The analysis stops before causal HR decisions

The results are descriptive signals for modeling and review, not automated hiring, firing, or employee evaluation rules.

Boundary: this article demonstrates an audit-to-modeling workflow. It does not claim causal drivers of attrition.

Evidence format: each code cell has a one-sentence functionality summary, code remains available for review, and repeated plot galleries are summarized with labeled outputs so the article stays readable.

Large tables and repeated figures are collapsible; key previews remain visible where they support the main claim.

Problem Statement

This analysis asks three practical questions:

  1. Which data-quality issues must be resolved before interpreting employee attrition?
  2. Which categorical and numerical employee features show the strongest marginal association with attrition?
  3. What modeling assumptions should be checked before moving from EDA to classification modeling?

The dataset is used for analytical and educational purposes only. The output should be read as descriptive workforce analysis, not as an automated decision system.

The first stage follows standard EDA practice: checking missing values, duplicated records, data types, and unusual feature ranges. However, missingness is not treated as a purely technical cleaning issue. If missing records are systematically related to attrition, tenure, department, or employee survival in the HR system, dropping them may introduce complete-case bias or survivorship-style sample selection bias, leading to misleading assumptions about the workforce.

Therefore, before imputation or deletion, I audit missingness patterns, compare retained vs dropped samples, add missingness indicators, and use conservative imputation strategies for modeling.

Step 01Prepare the environment for loading the Kaggle HR dataset.
Purpose

Install the Kaggle helper package only when the notebook is run in a fresh environment.

Code · Install Kaggle helper 
# Purpose: Install the Kaggle helper package in a fresh runtime.
!pip -q install kagglehub
Step 02Load libraries and configure readable dataframe output.
Purpose

Import the analysis stack and widen dataframe display so audit tables are easier to inspect.

Code · Load libraries 
# Purpose: Import the analysis stack and configure readable notebook output.
import os
import glob
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import kagglehub
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 100)
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 100)
Step 03Locate the downloaded HR Analytics data file.
Purpose

Search the Kaggle download folder for CSV or spreadsheet files before loading the dataset.

Code · Find dataset file 
# Purpose: Find the downloaded HR Analytics data file before loading it.
data_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith((".csv", ".xlsx", ".xls"))]
data_files
Step 04Load the raw HR table and confirm its structure.
Evidence · 2 items
Purpose

Read the detected file, report its shape, and preview the first rows to validate the raw table.

Code · Load and preview data 
# Purpose: Load the raw HR table and verify its shape and first rows.
dfs = {}

for f in data_files:
    name = os.path.basename(f)
    if f.endswith(".csv"):
        dfs[name] = pd.read_csv(f)
    else:
        dfs[name] = pd.read_excel(f)

for name, df in dfs.items():
    print(name, df.shape)
    display(df.head())
Raw table loadedConfirms the CSV loads correctly with 1,480 rows and 38 columns. 
HR_Analytics.csv (1480, 38)
Raw table previewShows the first employee records and verifies that target, job, salary, and tenure fields are present.

Step 05Standardize the working dataframe schema.
Purpose

Create the working dataframe and normalize column names for consistent downstream references.

Code · Standardize columns 
# Purpose: Normalize column names and create stable working dataframes.
df = list(dfs.values())[0].copy()

df.columns = (
    df.columns
    .str.strip()
    .str.replace(" ", "_")
    .str.replace("-", "_")
    .str.lower()
)
print("Shape:", df.shape)
Step 06Check exact duplicate rows before cleaning.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Count duplicated records so cleaning decisions are documented before any row is removed.

Code · Check duplicates 
# Purpose: Audit exact duplicate records before making cleaning decisions.
print("Duplicated rows:", df.duplicated().sum())
Exact duplicate auditFinds 7 fully duplicated rows before any conservative cleanup. 
Duplicated rows: 7
Step 07Audit missingness, data types, and cardinality.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Build a missing-value summary with counts, rates, dtypes, and unique-value counts for each feature.

Code · Missingness audit 
# Purpose: Summarize missingness, data types, and feature cardinality.
missing_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "missing_count": df_eda.isna().sum(),
    "missing_rate": df_eda.isna().mean(),
    "dtype": df_eda.dtypes.astype(str)
}).sort_values("missing_rate", ascending=False)

display(missing_summary[missing_summary["missing_count"] > 0])
Missingness profileShows that missingness is concentrated in YearsWithCurrManager: 57 cells, about 3.85%.
missing_count missing_rate dtype
yearswithcurrmanager 57 0.038514 float64
Step 08Test whether manager-tenure missingness relates to attrition.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Compare attrition patterns for missing versus observed manager-tenure values before imputation.

Code · Missingness vs attrition 
# Purpose: Check whether missing manager-tenure records have different attrition rates.
col = "yearswithcurrmanager"

missing_check = (
    df_eda
    .assign(was_missing=df_eda[col].isna().astype(int))
    .groupby("was_missing")["attrition_flag"]
    .agg(count="size", attrition_rate="mean")
)

display(missing_check)
Manager-tenure missingness checkCompares attrition rate for rows with and without missing manager-tenure values.
count attrition_rate
was_missing
0 1423 0.161630
1 57 0.140351
Step 09Create a model-ready copy with a missingness flag.
Purpose

Separate the modeling table from the EDA table and preserve manager-tenure missingness as a feature.

Code · Create model table 
# Purpose: Create a model table and preserve manager-tenure missingness as a feature.
df_model = df_eda.copy()
col = "yearswithcurrmanager"

df_model[f"{col}_was_missing"] = df_model[col].isna().astype(int)
Step 10Impute manager tenure with grouped medians.
Purpose

Fill manager-tenure values using role and department medians, with the global median as fallback.

Code · Impute manager tenure 
# Purpose: Impute manager tenure using role/department medians with a global fallback.
df_model[col] = df_model[col].fillna(df_model[col].median())
col = "yearswithcurrmanager"

group_cols = [c for c in ["jobrole", "department"] if c in df_model.columns]

if len(group_cols) > 0:
    df_model[col] = (
        df_model
        .groupby(group_cols)[col]
        .transform(lambda s: s.fillna(s.median()))
    )

# fallback: 如果某些 group 全缺失，再用全局 median 补
df_model[col] = df_model[col].fillna(df_model[col].median())
Step 11Re-check preserved missingness in the EDA table.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Confirm that df_eda still preserves original missingness for audit; imputation belongs in the model table.

Code · Verify missingness 
# Purpose: Confirm that the readable EDA table still preserves original missingness.
missing_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "missing_count": df_eda.isna().sum(),
    "missing_rate": df_eda.isna().mean(),
    "dtype": df_eda.dtypes.astype(str)
}).sort_values("missing_rate", ascending=False)

display(missing_summary[missing_summary["missing_count"] > 0])
Preserved EDA missingness checkConfirms the EDA table still exposes the original missing manager-tenure field for audit transparency.

Feature representation note

This dataset mixes categorical group labels and numerical employee attributes. For readable EDA, I keep labels such as department, job role, overtime, age group, and salary slab; for modeling, nominal categories should be one-hot encoded rather than label-encoded to avoid fake ordering.

Dimensionality reduction is treated as a future exploratory option, not as final evidence in this article.

Step 14Split features into numeric and categorical groups.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Separate variable types so each feature receives an appropriate plot and statistical treatment.

Code · Feature type split 
# Purpose: Split variables into numeric and categorical feature groups.
numeric_features = df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()
categorical_features = df.select_dtypes(exclude=np.number).columns.tolist()

non_analytical_preview = {
    "empid", "employeenumber", "employeecount",
    "over18", "standardhours", "attrition"
}

feature_type_preview = {
    "total_columns": df.shape[1],
    "numeric_examples": [
        c for c in numeric_features
        if c not in non_analytical_preview and df[c].nunique(dropna=True) > 1
    ][:10],
    "categorical_examples": [
        c for c in categorical_features
        if c not in non_analytical_preview and df[c].nunique(dropna=True) > 1
    ][:10]
}

feature_type_preview
Feature-type preview

The raw dataset has 38 total columns. This preview shows representative fields before identifiers, constants, the target, and non-analytical variables are filtered out for plots and modeling.

Step 15Detect the attrition target column.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Find the target field from common attrition-related column names before encoding it.

Code · Detect target 
# Purpose: Identify the attrition target column before encoding.
target_candidates = ["attrition", "left", "turnover", "resigned", "is_left"]

target = None
for col in df.columns:
    if col.lower() in target_candidates:
        target = col
        break

print("Detected target:", target)
Detected target columnVerifies that Attrition is the binary outcome used throughout the article. 
Detected target: attrition
Step 16Encode attrition as a binary modeling target.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Map employees who left to 1 and employees who stayed to 0 for later group comparisons and models.

Code · Encode target 
# Purpose: Encode employee attrition as a binary target flag.
df_eda = df.copy()

if df_eda[target].dtype == "object":
    df_eda["attrition_flag"] = (
        df_eda[target]
        .astype(str)
        .str.lower()
        .map({"yes": 1, "no": 0, "true": 1, "false": 0, "1": 1, "0": 0})
    )
else:
    df_eda["attrition_flag"] = df_eda[target].astype(int)

df_eda["attrition_flag"].value_counts(dropna=False)
Encoded target countsShows the 0/1 class counts after mapping Attrition into attrition_flag.
count
attrition_flag
0 1242
1 238

Step 17Summarize the attrition base rate and class imbalance.
Evidence · 2 items
Purpose

Compute and plot the target distribution so later results are interpreted against the baseline rate.

Code · Target base rate 
# Purpose: Summarize target imbalance and visualize the class base rate.
attrition_counts = df_eda["attrition_flag"].value_counts().sort_index()
attrition_rate = df_eda["attrition_flag"].mean()

print("Attrition rate:", round(attrition_rate, 4))

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 4))
sns.countplot(data=df_eda, x="attrition_flag")
plt.title("Overall Attrition Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Attrition Flag")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Baseline attrition rateReports the overall attrition base rate, about 16.1%, before modeling. 
Attrition rate: 0.1608
Target class-balance chartVisualizes the class imbalance so accuracy is not overinterpreted later.
Step 18Remove identifier fields from the analysis table.
Purpose

Drop ID-like columns so charts and association checks do not treat identifiers as predictive variables.

Code · Drop identifiers 
# Purpose: Remove identifier-like fields from analysis features.
drop_cols = [
    "empid",
    "employeeid",
    "employeenumber",
    "employee_number"
]

df_eda = df_eda.drop(columns=drop_cols, errors="ignore")
Step 19Normalize inconsistent business-travel labels.
Purpose

Clean label spelling so business-travel categories aggregate correctly in plots and summaries.

Code · Normalize travel label 
# Purpose: Normalize business-travel labels so categories aggregate correctly.
df_eda["businesstravel"] = df_eda["businesstravel"].replace({
    "TravelRarely": "Travel_Rarely"
})

Feature engineering note: zero-inflated tenure variables

Some tenure-related variables contain many zeros. Instead of treating this as a plotting nuisance, I create an explicit indicator so the model can preserve the information that an employee has not experienced a recent promotion while keeping the original tenure scale available for EDA.

Step 20Create a recent-promotion feature for tenure interpretation.
Purpose

Convert a zero-heavy promotion-tenure variable into a simpler binary descriptive signal.

Code · Promotion flag 
# Purpose: Create a binary recent-promotion indicator from a zero-heavy tenure field.
df_eda["no_recent_promotion_flag"] = (
    df_eda["yearssincelastpromotion"] > 3
).astype(int)

Insight

The attrition rate provides the baseline context for the entire analysis. A low attrition rate is not necessarily a data problem; it may reflect the normal base rate of employee turnover. However, it creates a modeling challenge because attrition cases may be underrepresented.

From an HR perspective, the goal is not only to maximize prediction accuracy, but to identify meaningful patterns among employees who actually leave, while avoiding overgeneralization from a small subgroup.

Step 21Define the categorical attrition-rate plotter.
Purpose

Create a reusable function for comparing group sample size and attrition rate across categorical features.

Code · Categorical plot helper 
# Purpose: Define a reusable table-and-chart diagnostic for categorical attrition rates.
def plot_attrition_rate_by_category(col, top_n=15):
    temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()

    if temp[col].nunique() > top_n:
        top_categories = temp[col].value_counts().head(top_n).index
        temp = temp[temp[col].isin(top_categories)]

    rate = (
        temp.groupby(col)["attrition_flag"]
        .agg(["mean", "count"])
        .sort_values("mean", ascending=False)
    )

    display(rate)

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
    sns.barplot(x=rate.index, y=rate["mean"])
    plt.title(f"Attrition Rate by {col}")
    plt.xlabel(col)
    plt.ylabel("Attrition Rate")
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
Step 22Select interpretable categorical variables for analysis.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Keep interpretable HR grouping variables while excluding identifiers, the target, and constant fields.

Code · Select non-constant categorical features 
# Purpose: Select interpretable, non-constant HR grouping variables for categorical EDA.

categorical_features = df_eda.select_dtypes(exclude=np.number).columns.tolist()

non_analytical_cols = {
    target,
    "empid",
    "employeenumber",
    "employeecount",
    "over18",
    "standardhours",
}

important_cat_cols = [
    col
    for col in categorical_features
    if col not in non_analytical_cols
    and 1 < df_eda[col].nunique(dropna=True) <= 30
]

important_cat_cols
Selected categorical scan

9 interpretable categorical variables are kept for the attrition-rate scan; identifiers, the target, and constant fields are excluded before plotting.

agegroupbusinesstraveldepartmenteducationfieldgenderjobrolemaritalstatussalaryslabovertime

Before making statistical inference, I first inspect the data graphically. Plots often reveal structure that is not obvious from summary statistics alone. For example, a Q-Q plot compares empirical quantiles with theoretical Gaussian quantiles, making it useful for diagnosing skewness, outliers, and heavy-tailed behavior. However, it should be interpreted as a visual diagnostic rather than a precise estimator of the tail decay rate.

Step 23Generate the categorical attrition-rate chart gallery.
Gallery · 18 evidence items
Purpose

Loop through selected non-constant categorical features; the useful evidence is the labeled table/chart output below.

Code · Generate gallery 
# Purpose: Run categorical attrition-rate diagnostics on selected non-constant HR groups.

for col in important_cat_cols:
    plot_attrition_rate_by_category(col)
Redundant code note The reusable plotting function is defined in the previous cell; this cell is just the batch runner, so its code is folded by default.

Overtime attrition-rate chartBar chart of group-level attrition rates; use it as a visual preview, not a causal claim.

Optional modeling note: dimensionality reduction

Employee records can be represented as high-dimensional vectors after encoding categorical variables and combining numerical features:

\[x_i \in \mathbb{R}^p.\]

A projection method would seek a lower-dimensional representation:

\[z_i = \phi(x_i), \qquad z_i \in \mathbb{R}^d,\quad d \ll p.\]

These methods are not used as final evidence here. They are mentioned only as future tools for visualizing encoded employee structure after the core EDA and baseline modeling steps are stable.

Step 24Define Wilson intervals for group attrition rates.
Purpose

Add uncertainty intervals so small groups are not overinterpreted from raw attrition rates alone.

Code · Wilson interval helper 
# Purpose: Define Wilson intervals for uncertainty-aware group attrition rates.
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency, norm
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_attrition_rate_by_category_with_confidence_interval(
    col,
    top_n=15,
    alpha=0.05,
    desired_moe_list=(0.05, 0.10)
):
    temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()

    if temp[col].nunique() > top_n:
        top_categories = temp[col].value_counts().head(top_n).index
        temp = temp[temp[col].isin(top_categories)]

    summary = (
        temp.groupby(col)["attrition_flag"]
        .agg(attrition_count="sum", total_count="count", attrition_rate="mean")
        .sort_values("attrition_rate", ascending=False)
    )

    summary["non_attrition_count"] = summary["total_count"] - summary["attrition_count"]

    # Wilson confidence interval
    ci_low, ci_high = proportion_confint(
        count=summary["attrition_count"],
        nobs=summary["total_count"],
        alpha=alpha,
        method="wilson"
    )

    summary["ci_low"] = ci_low
    summary["ci_high"] = ci_high
    summary["wilson_half_width"] = (summary["ci_high"] - summary["ci_low"]) / 2

    # Normal approximation / CLT-based standard error
    z = norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
    p_hat = summary["attrition_rate"]
    n = summary["total_count"]

    summary["normal_se"] = np.sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n)
    summary["normal_moe_95"] = z * summary["normal_se"]

    # CLT convergence proxy: error scale shrinks like 1 / sqrt(n)
    summary["clt_rate_proxy_1_over_sqrt_n"] = 1 / np.sqrt(n)

    # Operational sample size requirement for desired margin of error
    # Use global attrition rate as a practical reference.
    global_p = temp["attrition_flag"].mean()

    for moe in desired_moe_list:
        required_n = np.ceil((z ** 2) * global_p * (1 - global_p) / (moe ** 2)).astype(int)
        col_name = f"enough_for_{int(moe * 100)}pp_moe"
        summary[col_name] = summary["total_count"] >= required_n

    # A simple quality flag based on event count and CI width
    def quality_flag(row):
        min_class_count = min(row["attrition_count"], row["non_attrition_count"])

        if row["total_count"] < 30:
            return "unstable: n < 30"
        elif min_class_count < 5:
            return "unstable: very small event count"
        elif min_class_count < 10:
            return "caution: small event count"
        elif row["wilson_half_width"] > 0.10:
            return "caution: wide CI"
        else:
            return "reasonably stable"

    summary["estimation_quality"] = summary.apply(quality_flag, axis=1)

    display(summary)

    # Chi-square test for association
    contingency = pd.crosstab(temp[col], temp["attrition_flag"])
    chi2, p_value, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(contingency)

    n_total = contingency.to_numpy().sum()
    r, k = contingency.shape
    cramers_v = np.sqrt(chi2 / (n_total * min(r - 1, k - 1)))

    min_expected = expected.min()

    print(f"Chi-square test p-value for {col}: {p_value:.4g}")
    print(f"Cramer's V effect size for {col}: {cramers_v:.4f}")
    print(f"Minimum expected cell count: {min_expected:.2f}")

    if min_expected < 5:
        print("Warning: Some expected cell counts are below 5. The chi-square approximation may be unreliable.")

    for moe in desired_moe_list:
        required_n = np.ceil((z ** 2) * global_p * (1 - global_p) / (moe ** 2)).astype(int)
        print(f"Approximate n per group needed for ±{int(moe * 100)} percentage-point margin of error: {required_n}")

    # Plot with Wilson CI
    plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4))
    plt.errorbar(
        x=np.arange(len(summary)),
        y=summary["attrition_rate"],
        yerr=[
            summary["attrition_rate"] - summary["ci_low"],
            summary["ci_high"] - summary["attrition_rate"]
        ],
        fmt="o",
        capsize=4
    )

    plt.axhline(
        temp["attrition_flag"].mean(),
        linestyle="--",
        linewidth=1,
        label="Overall attrition rate"
    )

    plt.xticks(np.arange(len(summary)), summary.index, rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.title(f"Attrition Rate by {col} with Wilson 95% CI")
    plt.xlabel(col)
    plt.ylabel("Attrition Rate")
    plt.ylim(0, min(1, summary["ci_high"].max() + 0.05))
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    return summary

For each categorical group, the attrition rate is estimated as a binomial proportion:

\[\hat p_g=\frac{x_g}{n_g},\]

where \(x_g\) is the number of attrition cases and \(n_g\) is the group size.

By the central limit theorem, the standard error of this estimate is approximately:

\[SE(\hat p_g) \approx \sqrt{\frac{\hat p_g(1-\hat p_g)}{n_g}}.\]

Therefore, the estimation error decreases at rate \(O(1/\sqrt{n_g})\). Small groups may show high or low attrition rates simply due to sampling noise. For this reason, I report Wilson confidence intervals, event counts, and sample-size adequacy flags rather than interpreting raw attrition rates alone.

Step 25Generate uncertainty-aware categorical rate plots.
Gallery · 27 evidence items
Purpose

Plot non-constant categorical attrition rates with Wilson intervals to compare groups more responsibly.

Code · Wilson plots 
# Purpose: Run Wilson-interval diagnostics on selected non-constant HR groups.

for col in important_cat_cols:
    plot_attrition_rate_by_category_with_confidence_interval(col)

Approximate n per group needed for ±5 percentage-point margin of error: 208

Overtime interval chartShows group attrition rates with uncertainty intervals, preventing overreading small groups.

Summary of Categorical Attrition Analysis

The overall attrition rate is approximately 16.1%. Among categorical variables, overtime, job role, age group, marital status, salary slab, and business travel show the strongest marginal associations with attrition.

Overtime shows the largest observed marginal separation: employees who worked overtime have an attrition rate of 30.6%, compared with 10.4% for employees without overtime. This suggests workload pressure or burnout-related factors may be useful retention signals, but it is not causal proof.

Job role also shows a strong association with attrition. Sales Representatives and Laboratory Technicians have relatively high attrition rates, although some job-role categories require interval checks because of smaller sample sizes.

Age group shows a clear descriptive pattern: younger employees, especially those aged 18–25, have much higher attrition rates than mid-career groups. This may reflect early-career mobility or weaker organizational attachment.

Business travel, marital status, and salary slab also show meaningful associations, but they may proxy other factors such as age, tenure, job role, and career stage. Gender does not show a meaningful marginal association in this dataset.

Overall, these are marginal associations rather than causal effects. The next step is to fit stratified multivariate baselines and check whether these variables remain informative after controlling for salary, tenure, role, age, and travel.

Part 3: Numerical Feature Visualization

After analyzing categorical variables, I inspect numerical variables. Numerical features may include continuous variables such as age, income, distance from home, and years at company, as well as ordinal score variables such as job satisfaction, environment satisfaction, and work-life balance.

The goals of this section are:

  1. To inspect distributional shape, skewness, outliers, and heavy-tailed behavior.
  2. To compare numerical feature distributions between attrition and non-attrition groups.
  3. To identify numerical variables that show strong marginal association with attrition.
  4. To check whether Gaussian-based methods such as LDA are reasonable approximations.
Step 26Build a filtered numeric feature list.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Exclude identifiers, constants, and the target from numeric EDA candidate variables.

Code · Numeric feature list 
# Purpose: Build a numeric feature list excluding identifiers, constants, and the target.
numeric_features_all = df_eda.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()

exclude_numeric_cols = [
    "attrition_flag",
    "empid",
    "employeeid",
    "employee_number",
    "employeenumber",
    "employeecount",
    "standardhours"
]

numeric_features = [
    c for c in numeric_features_all
    if c not in exclude_numeric_cols and df_eda[c].nunique(dropna=True) > 1
]

numeric_features
Numerical visualization set

23 numeric variables are available for plotting; the article focuses on HR-interpretable variables such as age, income, distance, tenure, and satisfaction.

agedailyratedistancefromhomeeducationenvironmentsatisfactionhourlyratejobinvolvementjobleveljobsatisfactionmonthlyincomemonthlyratenumcompaniesworked
Step 27Separate continuous-like and ordinal-like numeric variables.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Classify numeric variables by measurement role so distribution plots are interpreted correctly.

Code · Numeric role split 
# Purpose: Separate continuous-like and ordinal-like variables before plotting.
continuous_numeric_features = [
    c for c in numeric_features
    if df_eda[c].nunique(dropna=True) > 10
]

ordinal_numeric_features = [
    c for c in numeric_features
    if 2 < df_eda[c].nunique(dropna=True) <= 10
]

binary_numeric_features = [
    c for c in numeric_features
    if df_eda[c].nunique(dropna=True) <= 2
]

print("Continuous-like numeric features:", continuous_numeric_features)
print("Ordinal-like numeric features:", ordinal_numeric_features)
print("Binary-like numeric features:", binary_numeric_features)
Continuous-like numeric variables

23 variables have enough distinct values for distribution diagnostics such as histograms, boxplots, Q-Q plots, and skewness checks.

agedailyratedistancefromhomehourlyratemonthlyincomemonthlyratepercentsalaryhiketotalworkingyearsyearsatcompanyyearsincurrentroleyearssincelastpromotionyearswithcurrmanager
Step 28Summarize numeric distributions and skewness.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Compute missingness, center, spread, range, cardinality, and skewness for numeric variables.

Code · Numeric summary 
# Purpose: Summarize numeric center, spread, missingness, skewness, and tails.
numeric_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "missing_rate": df_eda[numeric_features].isna().mean(),
    "n_unique": df_eda[numeric_features].nunique(dropna=True),
    "mean": df_eda[numeric_features].mean(),
    "median": df_eda[numeric_features].median(),
    "std": df_eda[numeric_features].std(),
    "min": df_eda[numeric_features].min(),
    "q25": df_eda[numeric_features].quantile(0.25),
    "q75": df_eda[numeric_features].quantile(0.75),
    "max": df_eda[numeric_features].max(),
    "skewness": df_eda[numeric_features].skew(),
    "kurtosis": df_eda[numeric_features].kurtosis()
}).sort_values("skewness", key=lambda s: s.abs(), ascending=False)

display(numeric_summary)

yearsincurrentrole 0.000000 19 4.228378 3.0 3.616020 0.0 2.00 7.00 18.0 0.918546 0.484756

Step 29Define the numeric distribution diagnostic plotter.
Purpose

Create a reusable histogram, boxplot, and group-summary diagnostic for numeric features.

Code · Numeric plot helper 
# Purpose: Define the numeric histogram, boxplot, and group-summary diagnostic.
def plot_numeric_distribution_by_attrition(col):
temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()
temp["attrition_label"] = temp["attrition_flag"].map({
0: "Stayed",
1: "Left"
})
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
sns.histplot(
data=temp,
x=col,
hue="attrition_label",
bins=30,
kde=True,
stat="density",
common_norm=False,
ax=axes[0]
)
axes[0].set_title(f"Distribution of {col} by Attrition")
axes[0].set_xlabel(col)
axes[0].set_ylabel("Density")
sns.boxplot(
data=temp,
x="attrition_label",
y=col,
ax=axes[1]
)
axes[1].set_title(f"{col} by Attrition")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Attrition Group")
axes[1].set_ylabel(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
summary = (
temp.groupby("attrition_label")[col]
.agg(["count", "mean", "median", "std", "min", "max"])
)
display(summary)
Step 30Generate numeric diagnostics for key HR features.
Gallery · 22 evidence items
Purpose

Compare attrition groups across age, income, commute, tenure, and satisfaction-related variables.

Code · Numeric diagnostics 
# Purpose: Run numeric diagnostics for age, income, commute, tenure, and satisfaction.
important_numeric_cols = [
    "age",
    "monthlyincome",
    "distancefromhome",
    "totalworkingyears",
    "yearsatcompany",
    "yearsincurrentrole",
    "yearssincelastpromotion",
    "yearswithcurrmanager",
    "jobsatisfaction",
    "environmentsatisfaction",
    "worklifebalance"
]

important_numeric_cols = [
    c for c in important_numeric_cols
    if c in numeric_features
]

for col in important_numeric_cols:
    plot_numeric_distribution_by_attrition(col)

Numerical Feature Interpretation

The numerical summaries show several consistent patterns. Employees who left tend to be younger, have lower monthly income, live farther from the workplace, and have fewer total working years, fewer years at the company, fewer years in the current role, and fewer years with the current manager.

Satisfaction-related variables also show lower average scores among employees who left, especially job satisfaction and environment satisfaction. However, these score variables are ordinal rather than truly continuous, so they should be interpreted as ordered survey indicators instead of Gaussian numerical variables.

Step 31Define Q-Q diagnostics for Gaussian-assumption checks.
Purpose

Create a helper that checks whether numeric variables look approximately Gaussian within each attrition group.

Code · Q-Q helper 
# Purpose: Define Q-Q diagnostics for Gaussian-assumption checks by attrition group.
import scipy.stats as stats

def qq_plot_numeric_by_attrition(col):
    temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()

    for g in sorted(temp["attrition_flag"].unique()):
        x = temp.loc[temp["attrition_flag"] == g, col]

        plt.figure(figsize=(5, 5))
        stats.probplot(x, dist="norm", plot=plt)
        plt.title(f"Q-Q Plot of {col} | Attrition = {g}")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()
Step 32Generate Q-Q plots for selected numeric variables.
Gallery · 12 evidence items
Purpose

Use Q-Q plots to support later discussion of LDA and Gaussian-style assumptions.

Code · Q-Q diagnostics 
# Purpose: Run Q-Q diagnostics for selected continuous-like variables.
qq_cols = [
    "age",
    "monthlyincome",
    "distancefromhome",
    "totalworkingyears",
    "yearsatcompany",
    "yearsincurrentrole"
]

qq_cols = [
    c for c in qq_cols
    if c in continuous_numeric_features
]

for col in qq_cols:
    qq_plot_numeric_by_attrition(col)

Years at company Q-Q plot · Left groupChecks Gaussian-like behavior and tail deviation for years at company among employees who left.
Years in current role Q-Q plot · Stayed groupChecks Gaussian-like behavior and tail deviation for years in current role among employees who stayed.
Years in current role Q-Q plot · Left groupChecks Gaussian-like behavior and tail deviation for years in current role among employees who left.

For strongly right-skewed variables, I compare the original scale with a log-transformed scale. This does not mean the log transform must be used in every model, but it helps diagnose whether extreme values dominate the distribution.

Step 33Define a log-transform comparison helper.
Purpose

Create a helper that compares original and log scales for strongly right-skewed variables.

Code · Log-transform helper 
# Purpose: Define a log-transform diagnostic for strongly right-skewed variables.
def compare_original_vs_log_distribution(col):
    temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()

    if (temp[col] < 0).any():
        print(f"{col} contains negative values. log1p is not appropriate.")
        return

    temp[f"log1p_{col}"] = np.log1p(temp[col])

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

    sns.histplot(temp[col], bins=30, kde=True, ax=axes[0])
    axes[0].set_title(f"Original {col}")

    sns.histplot(temp[f"log1p_{col}"], bins=30, kde=True, ax=axes[1])
    axes[1].set_title(f"log1p({col})")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
Step 34Compare skewed variables before and after log transform.
Evidence · 5 items
Purpose

Identify skewed features and test whether log scaling reduces visual dominance by extreme values.

Code · Skewness diagnostics 
# Purpose: Compare original and log-scaled views of highly skewed features.
skewed_candidates = [
    c for c in continuous_numeric_features
    if abs(df_eda[c].skew()) > 1
]

print("Highly skewed candidates:", skewed_candidates)

for col in skewed_candidates:
    compare_original_vs_log_distribution(col)
Skewed-variable candidate listLists variables with |skewness| > 1 selected for the log-transform diagnostic. 
Highly skewed candidates: ['monthlyincome', 'totalworkingyears', 'yearsatcompany', 'yearssincelastpromotion']
Monthly income log-transform diagnosticCompares original and log-scaled distributions to see whether a few large values dominate the scale.
Total working years log-transform diagnosticCompares original and log-scaled distributions to see whether a few large values dominate the scale.
Years at company log-transform diagnosticCompares original and log-scaled distributions to see whether a few large values dominate the scale.
Years since last promotion log-transform diagnosticCompares original and log-scaled distributions to see whether a few large values dominate the scale.

The skewness check identifies several right-skewed variables: monthly income, total working years, years at company, and years since last promotion. This is expected in HR data because income, tenure, and promotion-related variables often have long right tails. Most employees are concentrated in lower or middle ranges, while a smaller number of senior or long-tenured employees create extreme high values.

Categorical Effect Size Ranking

I rank categorical variables using Cramér’s V. Overtime and job role show the strongest marginal association with attrition, followed by age group, marital status, salary slab, and business travel. Gender shows little marginal association in this dataset.

Classification Framework

The EDA prepares the data for simple baseline classifiers rather than claiming a finished predictive system. Because attrition is imbalanced, the relevant evaluation metrics are recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC rather than accuracy alone.

BaselineWhy it is usefulAssumption or check
Majority baselineShows why accuracy is misleading in an imbalanced attrition task.Predicts no leavers, so recall for attrition is zero.
Logistic regressionProvides an interpretable discriminative baseline for estimating attrition probability.Needs encoded categories, imputation, and leakage checks.
LDATests whether a simple Gaussian class-conditional approximation is informative.Requires caution because several numerical variables are skewed.
FDA + logistic regressionUses a projection that emphasizes class separation before classification.Useful as an exploratory linear separation baseline, not a final decision rule.

Classification theory note

This section gives the mathematical context for the baseline classifiers; it explains assumptions rather than reporting final production-model performance.

The theoretically optimal decision rule for a classification problem is the Bayes classifier.

Let \(X\) denote the feature vector and \(Y\) denote the class label. For a \(K\)-class classification problem, the Bayes classifier assigns each observation to the class with the largest posterior probability:

\[g^\star(x)=\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{k=1,\ldots,K} P(Y=k \mid X=x).\]

For binary classification, where \(Y \in \{0,1\}\), the rule becomes:

\[g^\star(x)=1 \quad \text{if} \quad P(Y=1 \mid X=x)>0.5,\]

and otherwise:

\[g^\star(x)=0.\]

However, in real applications, the true data-generating distribution is unknown, so the Bayes classifier is usually unattainable. In practice, classification problems are commonly approached through several modeling strategies.

Generative classification methods model the joint distribution of features and labels by estimating the class prior \(\pi_k = P(Y=k)\) and the class-conditional density \(p(x \mid Y=k)\). Given these quantities, the posterior probability can be estimated through Bayes' rule:

\[P(Y=k \mid X=x) = \frac{\pi_k p(x \mid Y=k)} {\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j p(x \mid Y=j)}.\]

Linear Discriminant Analysis, or LDA, is a classical generative method. It assumes that each class follows a Gaussian distribution with a shared covariance matrix:

\[X \mid Y=k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k,\Sigma).\]

Under this assumption, the resulting decision boundary is linear.

Fisher's discriminant analysis takes a projection-based perspective. It seeks a direction \(w\) that maximizes between-class separation while minimizing within-class variation: (Mathematically, Fisher's criterion belongs to a common family of optimization problems known as generalized Rayleigh quotient problems.)

\[w^\star = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{w} \frac{w^\top S_B w}{w^\top S_W w}.\]

Here, \(S_B\) is the between-class scatter matrix and \(S_W\) is the within-class scatter matrix. In the binary case, the solution has the form:

\[w \propto S_W^{-1}(\mu_1-\mu_0).\]

Discriminative methods, such as logistic regression, model the posterior probability \(P(Y | X)\) directly. For binary classification, logistic regression assumes:

\[P(Y=1 \mid X=x) = \sigma(\beta_0+\beta^\top x) = \frac{1}{1+\exp[-(\beta_0+\beta^\top x)]}.\]

Another broad perspective is empirical risk minimization. Instead of explicitly estimating the true Bayes rule, we choose a function \(f\) from a model class by minimizing the empirical loss:

\[\hat f = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \ell(f(x_i),y_i) + \lambda \Omega(f).\]

Here, \(\ell\) is a loss function and \(\Omega(f)\) is a regularization term. Many models, including support vector machines, neural networks, and tree-based methods, can be understood under this framework.

These methods rely on different assumptions and are useful in different situations. In this HR analytics project, I use them as exploratory tools to understand which employee features are associated with attrition, rather than as automatic decision systems.

Step 35Define Cramér’s V for categorical association strength.
Purpose

Implement Cramér’s V to measure marginal association between categorical features and attrition.

Code · Cramér's V helper 
# Purpose: Define Cramér's V for categorical association strength.
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

def cramers_v_from_crosstab(table):
    chi2, p_value, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(table)
    n = table.to_numpy().sum()
    r, k = table.shape

    if min(r - 1, k - 1) == 0:
        return np.nan, p_value, chi2, dof, expected.min()

    v = np.sqrt(chi2 / (n * min(r - 1, k - 1)))
    return v, p_value, chi2, dof, expected.min()
Step 36Rank categorical variables by association with attrition.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Compute Cramér’s V for categorical variables and surface the strongest descriptive signals.

Code · Rank categorical effects 
# Purpose: Rank categorical variables by Cramér's V against attrition.
# Clean known inconsistent category label
if "businesstravel" in df_eda.columns:
    df_eda["businesstravel"] = df_eda["businesstravel"].replace({
        "TravelRarely": "Travel_Rarely"
    })

# Candidate categorical variables
categorical_features = df_eda.select_dtypes(exclude=np.number).columns.tolist()

effect_rows = []

for col in categorical_features:
    if col in ["attrition"]:
        continue

    # skip constant columns
    if df_eda[col].nunique(dropna=True) <= 1:
        continue

    temp = df_eda[[col, "attrition_flag"]].dropna().copy()

    contingency = pd.crosstab(temp[col], temp["attrition_flag"])

    if contingency.shape[1] < 2:
        continue

    v, p_value, chi2, dof, min_expected = cramers_v_from_crosstab(contingency)

    group_summary = (
        temp.groupby(col)["attrition_flag"]
        .agg(attrition_rate="mean", total_count="count")
    )

    effect_rows.append({
        "feature": col,
        "cramers_v": v,
        "p_value": p_value,
        "chi2": chi2,
        "dof": dof,
        "n_levels": temp[col].nunique(),
        "min_expected_count": min_expected,
        "min_group_size": group_summary["total_count"].min(),
        "max_group_size": group_summary["total_count"].max(),
        "min_attrition_rate": group_summary["attrition_rate"].min(),
        "max_attrition_rate": group_summary["attrition_rate"].max(),
        "attrition_rate_range": (
            group_summary["attrition_rate"].max()
            - group_summary["attrition_rate"].min()
        )
    })

effect_size_ranking = (
    pd.DataFrame(effect_rows)
    .sort_values("cramers_v", ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

display(effect_size_ranking)
Cramér's V ranking tableRanks categorical variables by marginal association with attrition and reports p-values and sample constraints.
feature cramers_v p_value chi2 dof n_levels min_expected_count min_group_size max_group_size min_attrition_rate max_attrition_rate attrition_rate_range
0 overtime 0.246288 2.670557e-21 89.773438 1 2 67.218919 418 1062 0.103578 0.306220 0.202642
1 jobrole 0.240390 3.752112e-15 85.525255 8 9 8.362162 52 329 0.025000 0.392857 0.367857
2 agegroup 0.199818 4.500285e-12 59.092236 4 5 7.558108 47 611 0.091295 0.357724 0.266428
3 maritalstatus 0.174578 1.604215e-10 45.106433 2 3 52.745946 328 679 0.103659 0.253700 0.150041
4 salaryslab 0.166650 6.219013e-09 41.102798 3 4 21.387838 133 753 0.037594 0.216467 0.178873
5 businesstravel 0.126981 6.577520e-06 23.863706 2 3 24.282432 151 1050 0.079470 0.247312 0.167842
6 educationfield 0.107336 4.404106e-03 17.050988 5 6 4.341892 27 607 0.132530 0.259259 0.126729
7 department 0.087392 3.511577e-03 11.303380 2 3 10.131081 63 967 0.137539 0.206667 0.069128
8 gender 0.028313 2.760484e-01 1.186442 1 2 95.039189 591 889 0.147208 0.169854 0.022646
Step 37Plot the categorical effect-size ranking.
Evidence · 1 item
Purpose

Visualize the Cramér’s V ranking so categorical attrition signals are easy to compare.

Code · Plot effect-size ranking 
# Purpose: Plot the Cramér's V ranking as a compact effect-size chart.
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.barplot(
    data=effect_size_ranking,
    x="cramers_v",
    y="feature"
)
plt.title("Categorical Feature Association with Attrition")
plt.xlabel("Cramér's V")
plt.ylabel("Feature")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Cramér's V ranking chartVisual summary showing which categorical variables have the strongest marginal association with attrition.

Limitations and Next Steps

This EDA does not identify causal drivers of attrition. Its value is to build a reproducible audit-to-modeling workflow: preserve the raw data, document duplicate and missingness issues, separate the readable EDA table from the model-ready table, and use uncertainty-aware group comparisons before fitting classifiers.

  • This article reports descriptive EDA summaries from the raw working table while documenting exact duplicates separately; the exact-deduplicated table should become the single source of truth before model fitting.
  • Fit stratified baseline models and report recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC.
  • Check whether overtime and job role remain informative after controlling for age, salary, tenure, travel, and satisfaction variables.
  • Confirm that identifiers, constants, derived bands, and duplicate-review fields do not leak information into the model.
  • Constant fields such as Over18 are documented in the audit but excluded from categorical evidence galleries because they carry no group-comparison signal.

Conclusion: the strongest portfolio signal in this project is not any single chart; it is the end-to-end discipline of auditing data quality, explaining uncertainty, separating EDA from modeling, and setting clear boundaries for HR analytics.

Contents Key findings Problem Statement Insight Summary of Categorical Attrition Analysis Part 3: Numerical Feature Visualization Numerical Feature Interpretation Categorical Effect Size Ranking Classification Framework Limitations and Next Steps
